title: Beyond Thirty: Treasury Issuance of Long-Term Bonds from 1953 to 1965

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This New York Fed Staff Report (Number 806, January 2017) by Kenneth D. Garbade examines historical Treasury bond issuance practices, specifically the seven long-term bonds issued between 1955 and 1963—including one with a forty-year term—before thirty years became the outer boundary of Treasury maturities in the 1970s. The source content is limited to a brief meta description; full paper content (36 pages) requires PDF access.
key_points: Seven long-term Treasury bonds were issued between 1955 and 1963, including one with a forty-year term
Thirty years became the outer boundary of Treasury maturities following the emergence of regular coupon-bearing debt issuance in the 1970s
Paper covers the period 1953 to 1965 and examines circumstances that led to issuance of these bonds
JEL classification: H63 (National Budget, Deficit, and Debt), N22 (Financial Markets and Institutions, U.S.)
Full paper available only in PDF format (36 pages, 268 kb)
why_it_matters: Understanding historical Treasury issuance practices provides context for the modern 30-year maturity convention and informs debates about potential future extensions of Treasury maturity issuance. This research supports understanding of how current market conventions were established.
macro_context: Historical Treasury issuance analysis from the 1950s-1960s period, prior to the establishment of the 30-year maturity standard in the 1970s.
market_structure_context: Historical analysis of Treasury issuance conventions; specifically examines how and why Treasury issuance maturities evolved, establishing context for modern 30-year maturity standard. Relevant to understanding Treasury market structure and issuance policy.
